docs.title: Mathematics: Analysis and Approaches with Ms Soh
docs.text:
Welcome to our Math website!

Interactive Explorations

IB Topical Questions and Solutions

docs.title: IB Topical Review and Examples
docs.text:
Welcome to the IB Topical Review and Examples section.

The SL MAA section is meant for students taking SL MAA and also students taking HL MAA.

The HL MAA section contains the additional HL content and is for HL students only.

docs.title: HL MAA Topical Review and Examples
docs.text:
In this part of the website, we do a review of key concepts and ideas from the additional HL MAA curriculum.

When using the worked examples, please attempt the questions on your own before looking at the solutions.

The content is organised according to the following strands:

1. Number and Algebra

2. Functions and Graphs

3. Geometry and Trigonometry

4. Calculus

5. Statistics and Probability

docs.title: Work in progress
docs.text: Contents:

docs.title: SL MAA Topical Review and Examples
docs.text:
In this part of the website, we do a review of key concepts and ideas from the SL MAA curriculum.

When using the worked examples, please attempt the questions on your own before looking at the solutions.

The content is organised according to the following strands:

docs.title: Work in progress
docs.text:
Contents:

1. Linear Functions and Graphs

2. Basic of Functions

3. Composite Functions

4. Inverse Functions

5. GDC Strategies for Graph Sketching

6. Quadratic Functions and Graphs

7. Quadratic Equations and the Nature of Roots

8. Quadratic Inequalities

9. Rational Functions

10. Transformations of Graphs

docs.title: Functions - Basic Concepts
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can determine if a given relation is a function either algebraically or using a vertical line test.
  • I can evaluate the value of a function at a given point.
  • I can find the domain and range of a given function.
  • I can explain what an inverse function is to my friend.

docs.title: Functions - Composite Functions
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can find the expression for \((f\circ g)\) given the functions \(f\) and \(g\).
  • I can state the domain of the composite function \((f\circ g)\).

docs.title: Functions - Quadratic Equations and Nature of Roots
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can solve a quadratic equation by factorisation.
  • I can solve a quadratic equation using the quadratic formula.
  • I can solve a quadratic equation by completing the square.
  • I can use the discriminant to determine the nature of roots of a given quadratic equation.
  • I can deduce the possible values of the discriminant based on the nature of roots of a given quadratic equation.

docs.title: Functions - GDC Skills for Graph Sketching
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can draw the graph of a function accurately on a given grid with the help of the GDC.
  • I can find the intercepts of a graph using the GDC.
  • I can deduce the asymptotes of a graph with the help of the GDC.
  • I can find the coordinates of maximum and minimum points of a graph using the GDC.
  • I can find the coordinates of the points of intersection between two graphs using the GDC.
  • I can solve equations using (i) graphical methods, (ii) polynomial solver, and (iii) system of linear equation solver on the GDC.

docs.title: Functions - Inverse Functions
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can determine if the function \(f^{-1}\) exists using an algebraic approach or the horizontal line test.
  • I can find the expression for \(f^{-1}\) given the function \(f\).
  • I can find the domain and range of \(f^{-1}\).
  • I can sketch the graph of \(f^{-1}\) given the graph of \(f\).

docs.title: Functions - Linear Functions and Graphs
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can find the gradient of a line using (i) its equation, and (ii) two points on the line.
  • I can state the relationship between the gradients of a pair of parallel lines.
  • I can state the relationship between the gradients of a pair of perpendicular lines.
  • I can find the equation of a straight line in gradient-intercept form \((y=mx+c)\), general form \((ax+by+c=0)\), and point-slope form \((y-y_1=m(x-x_1)\).
  • I can find the equation of the perpendicular bisector of a line segment.

docs.title: Functions - Quadratic Functions and Graphs
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can determine the vertex of a given quadratic function.
  • I can determine the axis of symmetry of a given quadratic function.
  • I can find the axial intercepts of a given quadratic function.
  • I can determine the concavity of a given quadratic function.
  • I can sketch the graph of a given quadratic function, from the forms \(y=ax^2+bx+c\), \(y=a(x-h)^2+k\) and \(y=a(x-p)(x-q)\).
  • I can find the equation of a quadratic function based on a sketch of its graph.

docs.title: Functions - Quadratic Inequalities
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can draw a sign diagram for a given function.
  • I can solve quadratic inequalities.

docs.title: Functions - Rational Functions
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can determine the vertical asymptote of graphs given in the form \(y=\dfrac{ax+b}{cx+d}\).
  • I can determine the horizontal asymptote of graphs given in the form \(y=\dfrac{ax+b}{cx+d}\).
  • I can sketch graphs given in the form \(y=\dfrac{ax+b}{cx+d}\).

docs.title: Functions - Transformations of Graphs
docs.text:
Back to Functions and Graphs

Progress: 0% Skill Checklist
  • I can (i) describe in words, (ii) sketch the graph, (iii) find the new equation of the graph obtained when \(y=f(x)\) undergoes the transformations \(y=f(x)+k\) and \(y=f(x+k)\).
  • I can (i) describe in words, (ii) sketch the graph, (iii) find the new equation of the graph obtained when \(y=f(x)\) undergoes the transformations \(y=pf(x)\) and \(y=f(qx)\), where \(p,\;q>0\).
  • I can (i) describe in words, (ii) sketch the graph, (iii) find the new equation of the graph obtained when \(y=f(x)\) undergoes the transformations \(y=-f(x)\) and \(y=f(-x)\).
  • I can solve problems involving a sequence of transformations.

docs.title: Interactive Explorations
docs.text:
Welcome to the Interactive Explorations section.

Over here, you can explore concepts from different parts of the curriculum with various applets.

1. Geometry and Trigonometry

docs.title: Geometry Explorations
docs.text:
Welcome to the Geometry Explorations section.

1. Connecting the sine graph to the unit circle

2. Connecting the cosine graph to the unit circle

3. Modelling a ferris wheel ride with trigonometric functions

docs.title: Deriving the Cosine Graph from the Unit Circle
docs.text:
This exploration investigates how the graphs of the cosine functions arise from the unit circle.

Increase the size of the angle \(s\) using the first button on the graph. When you think you have an idea of what the graph of \(y=\cos x\) looks like, click on the second button to reveal the graph.

Guiding Questions

THINK! 1. What happens to the \(x\)-coordinate of point \(P\) as the angle increases from \(0\) to \(\dfrac\pi2\)? 2. What value of \(s\) gives the highest possible \(x\)-coordinate? How does this relate to the graph of \(y=\cos x\)? 3. What is the amplitude of the graph of \(y=\cos x\)? Remember, the amplitude is the distance between the middle value and the highest or lowest point on the graph. 4. What is the period of the graph of \(y=\cos x\)? Remember, the period is the length (measured horizontally) of one complete cycle in a periodic graph.

When you are done exploring, click on the button below to see how the graph of \(y=\sin x\) is traced as \(x\) varies.

Click here to see the animated graph.

docs.title: Modelling Motion on a Wheel with Trigonometric Functions
docs.text:
This exploration investigates how the graphs of the sine and cosine functions can be used to model the path of a seat on a ferris wheel.

The screen below shows a rider on a ferris wheel, represented by the purple point. The moving point on the right shows the person's height above the ground at different points in time.

Guiding Questions

THINK! 1. How might you expect the graph of height against time to look? 2. What might the period of the graph represent in this case? 3. What might the amplitude of the graph represent in this case? 4. What is the relationship between the principal axis of the graph and the ferris wheel? 5. How do we decide on an appropriate function \(y=A\sin(B(x-C))+D\) or \(y=A\cos(B(x-C))+D\), and the values of \(A,\;B,\;C\) and \(D\)?

docs.title: Exploration 1
docs.text:
Show content

The screen below shows the path of a rider on a ferris wheel. Given that the height of the rider, \(h(t)\) at time \(t\) minutes from the beginning can be modelled with the equation \(h(t)=A\sin(Bt)+D\), find the values of \(A,\;B\) and \(D\).

docs.title: Exploration 2
docs.text:
Show content

The screen below shows the path of a rider on a ferris wheel. Given that the height of the rider, \(h(t)\) at time \(t\) minutes from the beginning can be modelled with the equation \(h(t)=A\cos(Bt)+D\), find the values of \(A,\;B\) and \(D\).

docs.title: Deriving the Sine Graph from the Unit Circle
docs.text:
This exploration investigates how the graphs of the sine functions arise from the unit circle.

Increase the size of the angle \(s\) using the first button on the graph. When you think you have an idea of what the graph of \(y=\sin x\) looks like, click on the second button to reveal the graph.

Guiding Questions

THINK! 1. What happens to the \(y\)-coordinate of point \(P\) as the angle increases from \(0\) to \(\dfrac\pi2\)? 2. What value of \(s\) gives the highest possible \(y\)-coordinate? How does this relate to the graph of \(y=\sin x\)? 3. What is the amplitude of the graph of \(y=\sin x\)? Remember, the amplitude is the distance between the middle value and the highest or lowest point on the graph. 4. What is the period of the graph of \(y=\sin x\)? Remember, the period is the length (measured horizontally) of one complete cycle in a periodic graph.

When you are done exploring, click on the button below to see how the graph of \(y=\sin x\) is traced as \(x\) varies.

Click here to see the animated graph.

docs.title: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions
docs.text:
We have learnt that the unit circle allows us to extend the use of trigonometry to angles that are non-acute. We will explore the relationship between the unit circle and the basic trigonometric graphs.

Graph of \(y=\sin x\)

Graph of \(y=\cos x\)

Trigonometric functions are also really useful in modelling situations with periodic or cyclic behaviour . A really common application is in modelling the height of a point on a ferris wheel.

Use of trigonometric graphs in modelling
